text: Porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have long been overlooked for electronic device applications, primarily due to the scarcity of frameworks that exhibit electrical conductivity. In this work, we demonstrate that conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs represent a powerful new class of materials for the chemiresistive sensing of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). We show that a sensor array constructed from a family of structurally analogous but chemically distinct 2D MOFs enables the clear discrimination of various VOC categories with high accuracy. Experimental evidence suggests that these materials operate through multiple, orthogonal sensing mechanisms—including charge transfer and potentially hydrogen bonding—distinguishing them from traditional chemiresistor materials. These results establish conductive MOFs as a highly tunable and mechanistically unique platform for next-generation sensory technologies.
